title: rachel barrie on craft cask and the future of scotch whisky
full_text: Main Eat Drink Reviews Insider At Home About Us Download Our Magazine Q2 2026 Q1 2026 Vol 1 Palate Dining Guide 2025-2026 Main Eat Drink Reviews Insider At Home About Us Download Our Magazine Q2 2026 Q1 2026 Vol 1 Palate Dining Guide 2025-2026 Now Reading: Rachel Barrie On Craft, Cask, And The Future Of Scotch Whisky 1 01 Rachel Barrie On Craft, Cask, And The Future Of Scotch Whisky Main// Eat// Drink// Reviews// Insider// At Home// About Us// Download Our Magazine// Q2 2026// Q1 2026// Vol 1 Palate Dining Guide 2025-2026// Home Insider Rachel Barrie On Craft, Cask, And The Future Of Scotch WhiskyRachel Barrie On Craft, Cask, And The Future Of Scotch WhiskyColin GomezInsider, Drink6 months agoShare With over 30 years at the sharp end of Scotch whisky, Rachel Barrie speaks on flavour, cask craft, and the responsibility of stewarding three historic distilleries. Rachel Barrie is one of the world’s foremost Master Blenders, so much so that she is often referred to as Scotch Whisky’s First Lady. With over three decades dedicated to the betterment of Scottish whisky, Rachel has plied her trade at distilleries as venerated as Glenmorangie, Ardbeg, and Bowmore. In her present capacity, she oversees the guardianship of the highest quality and the preservation of the unique identities of The Glendronach, Benriach, and Glenglassaugh’s Single Malt Scotch whiskies. Some of the feathers in what is becoming her increasingly weighty cap include the development of an award-winning range of Old & Rare coastal Single Malts for Glenglassaugh, including the distillery’s 50-year-old expression, as well as the stewardship of The Glendronach Aged 50 Years, the oldest and rarest expression to ever come out of the distillery in its nearly two centuries of existence. In this interview with Palate Asia, she touches on the intricacies that go into the making of some of the world’s finest expressions, as well as some thoughts on the industry as a whole. 1. What is one unwritten rule of traditional Scotch whisky-making you believe the industry must consciously hold onto? Traditional methods are important due to their fundamental effect on the character, quality, and authenticity of the spirit. The category is strictly defined by law to protect the spirit’s Protected Geographical Indication (PGI) status, which maintains the highest standards of quality and authenticity. Over and above the Scotch whisky definition and traditional methods, I think the industry must consciously hold onto the rich diversity in flavour and style found across the Scotch distilleries. I am lucky to work with three very diverse distilleries, from the ‘ben to the glen to the sea’, with Benriach, the elevated fruity and sweet Speyside, Glendronach, the rich and indulgent Highland, and Glenglassaugh, the luscious, tropical and coastal malt. This rich diversity of flavour within a relatively small geography is what attracts new and existing audiences to explore, experience, and discover the adventures of the Scotch whisky category. 2. Conversely, what is one that it must be willing to discard for the next generation to thrive? In order to thrive, I think the industry must discard the occasionally stuffy image, instead making the process and communication more ‘consumer-centric’ through exciting innovation, transparency, and most of all, relevance. We need to give the next generation a reason to be attracted to Scotch whisky, through sharing real and interesting stories about the process and people involved in making the whisky today, in a way that resonates and embraces sustainability, transparency, and even AI to reach the consumer. 3. What is one aspect of your role—be it a cask quirk, a seasonal challenge, or an industry shift—that still consistently surprises or challenges you, even after three decades? Changes in market demand (both up and down) continue to surprise and challenge me–if only I could see clearly into the future! Some whiskies are launched and sell double or even triple market forecasts, whilst others occasionally perform less well. Both create challenges for inventory, quality, and supply chain management. Having higher demand than predicted is a good problem to have, requiring constant reevaluation of demand curves to stay ‘ahead of the game’, building maturing inventory in the right wood at the right time, with the flexibility to dial up or down as the greatest market opportunities arise. 4. Beyond Oloroso and Pedro Ximénez, is there an underrated or less common cask type—perhaps a specific age, previous fill, or even wood type—that you believe holds the most underutilised potential for complex single malts? In recent years, I have introduced Amontillado, Palo Cortado, and Manzanilla sherry casks into the portfolios, where they have not been used before. Amontillado sherry is known as the most ‘complete’ sherry, and so it was only natural to use this cask type to mature Glendronach, to master and add to the PX and Oloroso sherry cask wood policy. The result of combining these three sherry cask styles at 30 years old has been exquisite, with many aficionados calling The Glendronach 30 years old ‘the best whisky they have ever tried’. Look out for a new expression of The Glendronach in 2026, incorporating Palo Cortado sherry casks, another rare and underrated cask type. I look forward to hearing what people think! 5. In creating a new expression, are you often seeking a “flavour destination” that you are guiding the spirit towards, or is it a more organic process where you are listening to what the casks are naturally trying to express? As with the best things in life, it all comes together perfectly when creating a new expression, whether by chance or by design, and usually a combination of both. Using experience, I tend to have a ‘flavour destination’ in mind, using flavour mapping to identify opportunities in which distillery character and the flavour profile can move into. For example, I map the four facets of The Glendronach character—robust, rich and fruity, refined, and full-bodied—and position each current expression and age on the map, before identifying ‘flavour opportunities’ that the distillery can naturally develop into, considering age, style (e.g., unpeated or peated), and maturation in different cask types. 6. What is the single most common misconception about the art of whisky blending that you wish you could correct for the average consumer? A common misconception is that Single malts are not “blended.” Many people confuse the terms “single malt” and “single cask”. A single malt comes from a single distillery but is typically a blend (or marriage) of many different casks, ages, and styles from that one distillery to ensure the perfect balance of flavour is consistently achieved in each and every bottle produced over time. A single cask expression, by contrast, is drawn from only one individual cask, and as such has an unrepeatable character, reflective of the character and flavour nuances at one moment after maturation in one cask. 7. A great single malt is often described as having a perfect balance. If balance is viewed not as a static point, but as a tension between two opposing forces (e.g., sweetness vs. dryness, spice vs. fruit), which two forces are the most challenging to hold in equilibrium in The Glendronach’s spirit? I believe the greatest opportunity in mastering balance and complexity lies in the tension and counterbalance between the different facets of distillery character as the whisky ages and matures in different cask types. The Glendronach has a counterpoise at every stage of its creation, from the robust Highland character balancing with the rich fruitiness of ‘the valley of the brambles’ in the spirit off the still, to the sweetness of Pedro Ximenez sherry casks counterbalancing the spicy dryness of Oloroso, and the influence of Spanish oak tannins in promoting the tension of both top note refinement as the casks breath, and the indulgent velvety texture. scotchwhiskyShare Leave a reply Cancel reply Save my name, email, and website in this browser for the next time I comment.
